title: Critical infrastructure resilience

compliance.assurance_rationale: NO KBF §6-1 — UC provides continuous Splunk telemetry evidence for this regulatory requirement, uplifted from contributing to partial after review confirmed the SPL captures relevant monitoring signals.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NO KBF §6-1 (Informasjonssikkerhet) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.26.10: Critical infrastructure resilience.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.26.10' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621)
spl:
index=itsi_summary source="itsi_grouped_alerts" earliest=-30d service_title="*Grid*"
| eval start=strptime(first_event_time,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval end=strptime(closed_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval mttr_min=round((end-start)/60,1)
| stats avg(mttr_min) as avg_mttr, perc95(mttr_min) as p95_mttr, count by service_title
| sort - p95_mttr
description: Links penetration test findings to remediation tickets to show resilience improvement cycles for electricity infrastructure.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NO KBF compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsi_summary. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders. Indexes required: `index=itsi_summary`.
visualization: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations). Key fields: `avg_mttr`, `end`, `mttr_min`, `p95_mttr`, `service_title`, `start`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux

knownFalsePositives: DSA reporting during scheduled control reviews, planned exception cycles, or after major regulatory changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=itsi_summary source="itsi_grouped_alerts" earliest=-30d service_title="*Grid*"
| eval start=strptime(first_event_time,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval end=strptime(closed_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval mttr_min=round((end-start)/60,1)
| stats avg(mttr_min) as avg_mttr, perc95(mttr_min) as p95_mttr, count by service_title
| sort - p95_mttr
```

Understanding this SPL

**Critical infrastructure resilience (RME/KI)** — Links penetration test findings to remediation tickets to show resilience improvement cycles for electricity infrastructure.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: itsi_summary.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=itsi_summary, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **start** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **end** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **mttr_min** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by service_title** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Critical infrastructure resilience (RME/KI)** — Links penetration test findings to remediation tickets to show resilience improvement cycles for electricity infrastructure.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Change.All_Changes` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the GRC system and the audit register, and with regional reporting cutoffs in your runbook. Document any expected regulatory reporting window or approved exception before escalating.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations).
grandmaExplanation: We help you show national security and critical-sector monitoring rules are met with the events you keep in one place for review.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Change (CIM data model).
